title: Metabolism — all the chemical reactions in your body
examTip: AQA expects you to know specific examples of anabolic and catabolic reactions — don't just define the terms, be ready to give examples like glycogen synthesis, urea formation, and protein synthesis. The liver's role in urea production is a common exam target.
introTips: This lesson is about seeing the body as a chemistry lab — hundreds of reactions happening simultaneously, all enzyme-controlled.
You'll learn the two categories of reactions: building up (anabolic) and breaking down (catabolic), with specific examples of each.
The urea pathway is one students often lose marks on — learn it precisely: excess amino acids → liver → urea → kidneys → excreted.
intro: Every second you're alive, thousands of chemical reactions are happening inside your cells. Glucose is being broken down, proteins are being assembled, fats are being built and disassembled, waste is being processed and removed. All of this together is called metabolism. It's controlled entirely by enzymes, and it's the reason you need to keep eating, breathing, and drinking — your body is a chemistry lab that never switches off.

keyPoints.heading: What metabolism is
keyPoints.content: Metabolism is defined as the sum of all chemical reactions occurring in the body. That 'sum of all' is important — it includes every reaction, not just respiration or digestion. All metabolic reactions are controlled by enzymes, which act as biological catalysts to speed them up. Your basal metabolic rate (BMR) is the minimum amount of energy your body needs when completely at rest — just to keep your heart beating, your lungs breathing, your body temperature stable, and your cells functioning. BMR varies between individuals based on age, sex, muscle mass, and body size. Higher muscle mass = higher BMR, because muscle tissue is metabolically more active than fat tissue.
writeBullets: Metabolism = the sum of ALL chemical reactions in the body
All metabolic reactions are controlled by enzymes
Basal metabolic rate (BMR) = energy needed at complete rest to sustain basic life processes
Higher muscle mass → higher BMR (muscle is more metabolically active than fat)
Metabolism includes both building-up (anabolic) and breaking-down (catabolic) reactions

keyTerms.def: The sum of all chemical reactions occurring in the body, controlled by enzymes
The minimum rate of energy expenditure needed to maintain basic body functions at rest
A biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions in the body without being used up

keyPoints.memTip: Metabolism = ALL reactions (anabolic + catabolic combined). BMR is the 'tick-over' energy use — the minimum needed just to stay alive at rest. When a question says 'define metabolism', your answer MUST include 'sum of all chemical reactions'. Not just respiration, not just digestion — ALL of them.

keyPoints.heading: Anabolic reactions — building up
keyPoints.content: Anabolic reactions are the building-up reactions — they synthesise large molecules from smaller ones. They always require energy as an input. The key examples AQA expects you to know are: glucose → glycogen (glucose molecules are joined together to form glycogen, a storage carbohydrate in the liver and muscles — this is how your body banks excess glucose); amino acids → proteins (amino acids are joined by peptide bonds, assembled according to the DNA code, to make all the structural and functional proteins your body needs — including enzymes, hormones, and muscle fibres); fatty acids + glycerol → lipids (fat molecules are built when glycerol is joined to fatty acid chains — lipids are used for cell membranes, energy storage, and insulation).
writeBullets: Anabolic = BUILDING UP (small molecules → large molecules); requires energy
Glucose → glycogen (storage carbohydrate, stored in liver and muscles)
Amino acids → proteins (structural proteins, enzymes, hormones, muscle)
Fatty acids + glycerol → lipids (cell membranes, energy storage, insulation)
All anabolic reactions are endothermic — they need an energy input

keyTerms.def: A metabolic reaction that builds up large molecules from smaller ones, requiring energy
A storage polysaccharide made from glucose; stored in the liver and muscles
The assembly of amino acids into protein chains, directed by DNA — an anabolic process
A fat or oil molecule made from fatty acids and glycerol; used for energy storage and cell membranes
The chemical bond linking amino acids together in a protein chain

keyPoints.memTip: Anabolic = ADDING (think 'anabolic steroids' — they build muscle). Three examples: G-A-L — Glycogen synthesis, Amino acid → protein, Lipid synthesis. Write them in a triangle if it helps. For each one, know: what's the small molecule? What's the large molecule it becomes? Where is it stored?

keyPoints.heading: Catabolic reactions — breaking down
keyPoints.content: Catabolic reactions are the breaking-down reactions — they break large molecules into smaller ones, often releasing energy. The three key examples for AQA: Respiration breaks down glucose → carbon dioxide + water (aerobic), releasing energy for the cell. This is the most important catabolic reaction in the body. Breakdown of excess amino acids (deamination): your body can't store amino acids. Any that aren't needed for protein synthesis are sent to the liver, where the amino group is removed in a process called deamination. The amino group is converted to urea, which travels in the blood to the kidneys and is excreted in urine. The remaining carbon skeleton enters respiration. Glycogen → glucose when blood sugar levels fall — glycogen stores are broken back down to release glucose back into the blood.
writeBullets: Catabolic = BREAKING DOWN (large molecules → small molecules); often releases energy
Respiration: glucose → CO₂ + water (releases energy — the main catabolic reaction)
Excess amino acids → deamination in LIVER → urea produced
Urea travels in blood to KIDNEYS → excreted in urine
Glycogen → glucose when blood sugar is low (reverse of the anabolic direction)

keyTerms.def: A metabolic reaction that breaks down large molecules into smaller ones, often releasing energy
The removal of the amino group from an amino acid in the liver; produces urea as a waste product
The nitrogenous waste product formed in the liver from excess amino acids; excreted by the kidneys in urine
The breakdown of glycogen back to glucose when blood sugar levels are low

keyPoints.memTip: For urea, learn the exact pathway: excess amino acids → LIVER (deamination) → urea → blood → KIDNEYS → urine. That pathway is worth 3–4 marks if you get it right. The common mistake is skipping the liver — always include it. Catabolism breaks things apart: think 'catastrophe' breaking things down.

1.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of metabolism?
options: The process of breaking down glucose to release energy
The sum of all chemical reactions occurring in the body
The rate at which the body uses energy during exercise
The digestion of food in the stomach and small intestine
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — metabolism is the sum of ALL chemical reactions in the body, including both building-up and breaking-down reactions.
1.wrongFeedback: Respiration is one part of metabolism, but metabolism is much broader. It is the sum of ALL chemical reactions in the body — both building-up reactions (anabolic) and breaking-down reactions (catabolic). None of the other options capture all of that.

2.question: In which organ is urea produced from excess amino acids?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — urea is produced in the liver through deamination of excess amino acids. The kidneys then filter it from the blood and excrete it in urine.
2.wrongFeedback: The kidneys excrete urea, but they don't make it. Urea is produced in the LIVER through a process called deamination — where the amino group is removed from excess amino acids. The urea then travels in the blood to the kidneys and leaves the body in urine.

commonMistakes: Saying metabolism only means respiration or digestion — metabolism is ALL chemical reactions, both anabolic and catabolic. Define it fully.
Confusing anabolic and catabolic — anabolic = building UP (small → large, needs energy); catabolic = breaking DOWN (large → small, releases energy). Learn these directions firmly.
Saying the kidneys produce urea — they don't. The liver produces urea (via deamination); the kidneys filter it from blood and excrete it. Two different organs, two different jobs.

Forgetting that glycogen can go in both directions — glucose → glycogen is anabolic (after a meal, storing excess); glycogen → glucose is catabolic (when blood sugar is low). The same molecules, opposite directions.
examTips: For any question about urea, use the three-word sequence: liver → blood → kidneys. Deamination happens in the liver; urea travels in the blood; kidneys excrete it in urine. Each of those three steps is likely a mark.

When giving examples of anabolic reactions, always name both the small molecule(s) and the large molecule produced — e.g. 'fatty acids and glycerol are joined to form lipids'. Just saying 'lipids are made' is too vague for exam marks.

BMR questions may appear in the context of diet and obesity topics. Key point: higher muscle mass = higher BMR; as people age, BMR tends to decrease. Link BMR to energy balance if the question is about body weight.

revisionCardBullets: Metabolism = the SUM OF ALL chemical reactions in the body; all controlled by enzymes
BMR = minimum energy needed at complete rest; higher muscle mass → higher BMR
Anabolic = BUILDING UP (small → large); requires energy input
Anabolic examples: glucose → glycogen; amino acids → proteins; fatty acids + glycerol → lipids
Catabolic = BREAKING DOWN (large → small); often releases energy
Catabolic examples: glucose → CO₂ + water (respiration); glycogen → glucose (when blood sugar falls)
Excess amino acids → deamination in LIVER → urea produced
Urea → blood → KIDNEYS → excreted in urine
